statement: Iceland, Norway, and Liechtenstein submitted a joint EEA EFTA comment on the DSA legislative proposal emphasising: (1) maintaining conditional liability exemptions for online intermediaries; (2) preserving the prohibition on general monitoring obligations; (3) protecting editorial media content from platform interference; (4) stronger protections for minors against micro-targeted advertising; and (5) balancing content removal obligations with freedom of expression. Norway's national implementation (Digitalytelsesloven) had consultation closing October 2025, targeting summer 2026 implementation.
source_name: EFTA — EEA EFTA States Comment on the Digital Services Act
caveats: The joint comment was submitted in 2021 during the legislative process. Final DSA text may differ from the version commented on. Norway's implementation timeline may have shifted since the consultation period.

related_entries.statement: The Digital Services Act (EU Regulation 2022/2065) establishes a tiered content moderation framework: all services must handle illegal content reports and explain removals; online platforms must conduct risk assessments and publish transparency reports; very large platforms (>45M EU users) face systemic risk assessments and independent audits. The DSA defines obligations only for illegal content — 'harmful but legal' content is explicitly excluded from removal obligations to protect freedom of expression. Non-compliance penalties reach up to 6% of global annual turnover.

EES-samningurinn veitir EFTA-ríkjum formlega aðild að sérfræðinganefndum framkvæmdastjórnar ESB (greinar 99–102) þar sem þau geta mótað löggjöf áður en hún er samþykkt en þau hafa hvorki atkvæðisrétt í ráðherraráðinu né Evrópuþinginu. Norsk ríkisstjórnargreinargerð frá 2012 (NOU 2012:2 «Utenfor og innenfor») komst að þeirri niðurstöðu að Noregur hefur þátttöku í um 500 sérfræðingahópum framkvæmdastjórnarinnar en áhrif á lokaniðurstöður löggjafar eru «takmörkuð».
